text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 28 August 2018

Dr Amrita Patel
Riverside Health Centre

12 Millbank Close
Southwark
London SE1 7TG
Dear Dr Amrita Patel
Hannah Cooper, DOB: 03-02-1987, Hospital No: H937462 NHS No. 7482916503
Flat 12, 5A Warwick Street, London W1B 5NE

Diagnoses/summary: Ongoing seizure disorder under review; implanted VNS in situ; no VNS parameter changes at today’s review. Prior investigations and semiology remain non-specific.

Neuroimaging/EEG: Previous studies not clearly localising; no new imaging requested today.

Medication history: Trials previously include levetiracetam and sodium valproate (both discontinued due to side effects). Currently taking lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily. Uses PRN buccal midazolam issued by community team (not used recently). No known drug allergies.

Cardiorespiratory: Reports occasional palpitations; baseline ECG earlier this year normal. VNS pocket site well healed; lead pathway non-tender.

Social/functional: Lives with partner; works part-time in retail. Sleep variable; trying to keep regular routine. Avoids alcohol; stays hydrated. Driving not undertaken and DVLA position understood. Mood described as stable though fatigued after poor sleep. ï¿½

Review today: She attended alone and brought her diary. She feels overall stability since the VNS was implanted, with fewer post-event headaches. Last seizure on 27 May as per diary entry; there have been no clearly documented events since then, and overall frequency is uncertain as some spells may represent panic or presyncopal episodes rather than definite seizures. She uses the VNS magnet at prodromal sensations with mixed effect. No adverse effects from VNS beyond mild throat pulling at higher stimulation bursts, which is tolerable. Appetite and weight stable. No new neurological complaints. Ã‚Â·

VNS: Device interrogated; battery status satisfactory; impedances within expected range. No parameter changes at this review. We rehearsed magnet use and safety. â€

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily; no dose change today.
- Maintain seizure and symptom diary; we discussed distinguishing features of panic/presyncope vs probable epileptic events as patient-reported markers.
- No VNS adjustments today; will reconsider at next visit if diary suggests recurrent events or increased clusters.
- Routine bloods via GP: FBC, U&E, LFTs within the next 2–3 months for general monitoring (patient agreeable).
- Safety advice reinforced (bathing/showering, heights, cooking). Provided written information leaflet.
- Follow-up in clinic in 6 months or sooner if events recur; patient may contact epilepsy specialist nurse for interim support. 

Thank you for your ongoing care. Please let me know if there are any concerns. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Last seizure on 27 May as per diary entry; there have been no clearly documented events since then, and overall frequency is uncertain as some spells may represent panic or presyncopal episodes rather than definite seizures.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter discusses seizures and provides timing of the last event: "Last seizure on 27 May as per diary entry; there have been no clearly documented events since then" with clinic date 28 August 2018. That is approximately 3 months seizure-free, but the patient must be seizure-free for at least 6 months to be labeled seizure free. Overall frequency is also stated as uncertain due to possible non-epileptic spells. Since a specific numeric frequency cannot be determined and the 6‑month seizure-free criterion is not met, the correct label is "unknown."
seizure_frequency_number: Last seizure on 27 May as per diary entry; there have been no clearly documented events since then, and overall frequency is uncertain as some spells may represent panic or presyncopal episodes rather than definite seizures.
